#  Department of Industrial Relations Careers 
Start your career with the Department of Industrial Relations (DIR). Our mission is to improve working conditions in California. DIR team members are leading a diverse, fast-paced and innovative department that serves workers and employers in California. 
Do you want to make a difference in your community? We have careers in many different fields for entry-level and experienced candidates.
  

  * [Career Fairs and Webinars](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/#Career-Fairs-and-Webinars)
  * [Benefits](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/#Benefits)
  * [Current Openings ](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/#Current-Openings)
  * [Apply Now](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/#Apply-Now)
  * [Resources](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/#Resources)


[![DIR jobs](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Videos.png)](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/Include/Videos.html)
**[Watch Our Videos](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/Include/Videos.html)**  
See what makes DIR one of the best places to work in California 
##  Career Fairs and Webinars
﻿   
| June 18, 2026:   |  [Unlocking Interview Success: Navigating the California State Hiring Process](https://events.gcc.teams.microsoft.com/event/14a86621-de17-4b1f-8d39-05d10fa2dbb8@02d0040e-1348-4c5e-86e4-0a5ae8ede9d6)
  * Virtual, 11am-12pm

 |  
| --- | --- |  
| June 25, 2026:   |  [Virtual Webinar: Become a Competitive Candidate - Understanding the State's Process](https://events.gcc.teams.microsoft.com/event/1061c42e-8e73-4dd6-a70d-b7f8702b72f8@02d0040e-1348-4c5e-86e4-0a5ae8ede9d6)
  * Virtual, 10am-11:30pm

 |  
Meet our team at a career fair or webinar. Learn about current openings and how to complete the state’s hiring process. 
Find a Career at DIR
![CCA-pic](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Administrative-Office-support.jpg)
### Administrative Office Support
Provide analytical, clerical or technical support to DIR employees in areas such as Human Resources, Accounting, Business Services, Contracts and Procurement and more.
![Amusement Ride Safety Engineer](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Amusement-Ride%20Safety-Engineer.png)
### Amusement Ride Safety Engineer
Protect the public by inspecting and issuing permits for permanent and portable amusement rides and tramways.
![Apprenticeship Consultant](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Apprenticeship-Consultant.jpg)
### Apprenticeship Consultant
Consult with employers to develop apprenticeship programs for a skilled workforce. Work with stakeholders to promote apprenticeship as a viable career pathway.
![Attorney](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Attorney.png)
### Attorney
Join our team of innovative legal professionals helping with some of the most advanced labor laws in the country. Represent DIR in workers’ compensation cases, civil litigation, provide legal advice on public works, prevailing wages, pending legislation or proposed regulations and more.
![Deputy Labor Commissioner](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Deputy-Labor-Commissioner.png)
### Deputy Labor Commissioner
Combat wage theft by enforcing labor laws and ensuring workers in California are paid properly. Participate in targeted enforcement to combat the underground economy and identify labor law violations.
![Elevator Safety Engineer](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Elevator-Safety-Engineer.JPG)
### Elevator Safety Engineer
Protect workplaces and the public by inspecting and issuing permits for conveyances like elevators and escalators.
![Hearing reporter](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Hearing-Reporter.png)
### Hearing Reporter
Enjoy a flexible work scheduleand other benefitsby working as a hearing reporter that records official proceedings related to workers’ compensation claims.
![Human Resources](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Human-Resources.jpg)
### Human Resources
Help DIR build and retain a diverse workforce. Assist employees with personnel related matters, administer payroll and benefits and support the work of management or programs.
![Industrial Hygienist](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Industrial-Hygienist.png)
### Industrial Hygienist
Protect workers from health hazards. Conduct inspections, investigations and sampling to prevent hazards by evaluating environmental conditions at workplaces.
![Information Technology](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Information-Technology.jpg)
### Information Technology
Provide support of systems and services through the IT help desk. Provide management and oversight of IT-related projects. Develop and maintain software, cloud-based systems and telecommunication.
![Legal Support](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Legal-Support.png)
### Legal Support
Join our team of innovative legal professionals. Legal support team members work with attorneys representing DIR in workers’ compensation cases, civil litigation, with legal advice on public works, prevailing wages, pending legislation or proposed regulations and more.
![Management Supervisor or Specialist](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Management-Supervisor-Specialist.jpg)
### Management Supervisor or Specialist
If you enjoy leading others, DIR has manager and supervisor careers. There are opportunities in the general Staff Services Manager series and in specialized classifications including Safety Engineer, Deputy Labor Commissioner and more [posted on the CalCareers website](https://www.jobs.ca.gov/CalHRPublic/Search/JobSearchResults.aspx#depid=83&view=Grouped).
![Mining and Tunneling Safety Engineer](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Mining-Tunneling-Safety-Engineer.png)
### Mining and Tunneling Safety Engineer
Work with the unit responsible for investigating mines and tunnels for workplace safety and health hazards.
![Outreach Liaison](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Outreach-Liaison.png)
### Outreach Liaison
Use bilingual skills and community engagement to help DIR inform and educate the public about labor laws and workplace requirements. There are positions dedicated to outreach, and opportunities to do community outreach in your regular work while pursuing another career with DIR.
![Safety Engineer at work.](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Safety-Engineer.png)
### Safety Engineer
Protect workers from safety hazards on the job. Conduct inspections and investigations of accidents and complaints to prevent hazards by identifying violations and issuing corrective action plans.
![Workers' Compensation](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Workers-Compensation.png)
### Workers' Compensation
Help ensure injured workers get benefits to recover and return to work. Monitor the administration of workers compensation claims, provide support and judicial services in an effort to resolve disputes related to workers’ compensation benefits.
[Back to top](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/#top)
##  Benefits
### Health, Wellness and Career Benefits
We invest in our team by offering generous benefits for health and retirement, and professional development opportunities to grow your career. Additional information on benefits can be found on the [CalHR website](https://www.calhr.ca.gov/employees/pages/salary-and-benefits.aspx).
[](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/)
  * [Health](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/)
  * [Insurance](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/)
  * [Benefits](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/)
  * [Retirement](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/)


## Health
  * Medical
  * Dental 


  * Vision
  * Employee Assistance Program 


## Insurance
  * Life 
  * Group Legal 
  * Long Term Care 


  * Long Term Disability
  * State Disability 


## Benefits
  * Vacation and Sick Leave
  * Paid Holidays
  * Professional Development Days
  * Employee Wellness


  * Bilingual Pay Differential
  * Relocation Reimbursement
  * Commute Subsidy


## Retirement
  * CalPERS Retirement 


  * 401K & 457(b) Plans


### Local Impact 
Make a difference in your community with a career at DIR. We serve all of California with over 50 offices around the state. 
### Our Values
DIR’s [Core Values](https://dir.ca.gov/DirectorsOffice/Strategic-Plan.pdf) are essential to our mission to improve working conditions in California. DIR values accountability, integrity, compassion, diversity, service and innovation.
### A Workplace for All
At DIR, we cultivate a diverse and inclusive work environment that is reflective of the people we serve. We actively encourage a workplace in which all employees are respected and valued, can contribute their full and unique talents and are recognized for their contributions to DIR.
Team members have the opportunity to participate in groups such as our monthly **Diversity and Inclusion Committee** and **Disability Advisory Committee** meetings, events and more to help support our diverse work environment.
### Career Development 
DIR has openings for entry-level and experienced candidates to support our mission. Team members are encouraged to participate in training to improve their skills and career development. 
Careers for inspectors, investigators, analysts, supervisors and more are available in all of DIR’s activities, including: 
  * Workplace Safety Inspections 
  * Labor Law Investigations 
  * Assistance to Injured Workers
  * Registering Apprenticeship Programs
  * Attorneys, Judges, Hearing Reporters, Legal Support 
  * Office Support, Human Resources, Information Technology 
  * and more! 


[Back to top](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/#top)

  2. Department of Industrial Relations Careers 


#  Department of Industrial Relations Careers 
Start your career with the Department of Industrial Relations (DIR). Our mission is to improve working conditions in California. DIR team members are leading a diverse, fast-paced and innovative department that serves workers and employers in California. 
Do you want to make a difference in your community? We have careers in many different fields for entry-level and experienced candidates.
  

  * [Career Fairs and Webinars](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm#Career-Fairs-and-Webinars)
  * [Benefits](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm#Benefits)
  * [Current Openings ](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm#Current-Openings)
  * [Apply Now](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm#Apply-Now)
  * [Resources](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm#Resources)


[![DIR jobs](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Videos.png)](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/Include/Videos.html)
**[Watch Our Videos](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/Include/Videos.html)**  
See what makes DIR one of the best places to work in California 
##  Career Fairs and Webinars
﻿   
| June 18, 2026:   |  [Unlocking Interview Success: Navigating the California State Hiring Process](https://events.gcc.teams.microsoft.com/event/14a86621-de17-4b1f-8d39-05d10fa2dbb8@02d0040e-1348-4c5e-86e4-0a5ae8ede9d6)
  * Virtual, 11am-12pm

 |  
| --- | --- |  
| June 25, 2026:   |  [Virtual Webinar: Become a Competitive Candidate - Understanding the State's Process](https://events.gcc.teams.microsoft.com/event/1061c42e-8e73-4dd6-a70d-b7f8702b72f8@02d0040e-1348-4c5e-86e4-0a5ae8ede9d6)
  * Virtual, 10am-11:30pm

 |  
Meet our team at a career fair or webinar. Learn about current openings and how to complete the state’s hiring process. 
Find a Career at DIR
![CCA-pic](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Administrative-Office-support.jpg)
### Administrative Office Support
Provide analytical, clerical or technical support to DIR employees in areas such as Human Resources, Accounting, Business Services, Contracts and Procurement and more.
![Amusement Ride Safety Engineer](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Amusement-Ride%20Safety-Engineer.png)
### Amusement Ride Safety Engineer
Protect the public by inspecting and issuing permits for permanent and portable amusement rides and tramways.
![Apprenticeship Consultant](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Apprenticeship-Consultant.jpg)
### Apprenticeship Consultant
Consult with employers to develop apprenticeship programs for a skilled workforce. Work with stakeholders to promote apprenticeship as a viable career pathway.
![Attorney](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Attorney.png)
### Attorney
Join our team of innovative legal professionals helping with some of the most advanced labor laws in the country. Represent DIR in workers’ compensation cases, civil litigation, provide legal advice on public works, prevailing wages, pending legislation or proposed regulations and more.
![Deputy Labor Commissioner](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Deputy-Labor-Commissioner.png)
### Deputy Labor Commissioner
Combat wage theft by enforcing labor laws and ensuring workers in California are paid properly. Participate in targeted enforcement to combat the underground economy and identify labor law violations.
![Elevator Safety Engineer](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Elevator-Safety-Engineer.JPG)
### Elevator Safety Engineer
Protect workplaces and the public by inspecting and issuing permits for conveyances like elevators and escalators.
![Hearing reporter](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Hearing-Reporter.png)
### Hearing Reporter
Enjoy a flexible work scheduleand other benefitsby working as a hearing reporter that records official proceedings related to workers’ compensation claims.
![Human Resources](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Human-Resources.jpg)
### Human Resources
Help DIR build and retain a diverse workforce. Assist employees with personnel related matters, administer payroll and benefits and support the work of management or programs.
![Industrial Hygienist](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Industrial-Hygienist.png)
### Industrial Hygienist
Protect workers from health hazards. Conduct inspections, investigations and sampling to prevent hazards by evaluating environmental conditions at workplaces.
![Information Technology](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Information-Technology.jpg)
### Information Technology
Provide support of systems and services through the IT help desk. Provide management and oversight of IT-related projects. Develop and maintain software, cloud-based systems and telecommunication.
![Legal Support](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Legal-Support.png)
### Legal Support
Join our team of innovative legal professionals. Legal support team members work with attorneys representing DIR in workers’ compensation cases, civil litigation, with legal advice on public works, prevailing wages, pending legislation or proposed regulations and more.
![Management Supervisor or Specialist](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Management-Supervisor-Specialist.jpg)
### Management Supervisor or Specialist
If you enjoy leading others, DIR has manager and supervisor careers. There are opportunities in the general Staff Services Manager series and in specialized classifications including Safety Engineer, Deputy Labor Commissioner and more [posted on the CalCareers website](https://www.jobs.ca.gov/CalHRPublic/Search/JobSearchResults.aspx#depid=83&view=Grouped).
![Mining and Tunneling Safety Engineer](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Mining-Tunneling-Safety-Engineer.png)
### Mining and Tunneling Safety Engineer
Work with the unit responsible for investigating mines and tunnels for workplace safety and health hazards.
![Outreach Liaison](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Outreach-Liaison.png)
### Outreach Liaison
Use bilingual skills and community engagement to help DIR inform and educate the public about labor laws and workplace requirements. There are positions dedicated to outreach, and opportunities to do community outreach in your regular work while pursuing another career with DIR.
![Safety Engineer at work.](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Safety-Engineer.png)
### Safety Engineer
Protect workers from safety hazards on the job. Conduct inspections and investigations of accidents and complaints to prevent hazards by identifying violations and issuing corrective action plans.
![Workers' Compensation](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/IMAGE/Workers-Compensation.png)
### Workers' Compensation
Help ensure injured workers get benefits to recover and return to work. Monitor the administration of workers compensation claims, provide support and judicial services in an effort to resolve disputes related to workers’ compensation benefits.
[Back to top](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm#top)
##  Benefits
### Health, Wellness and Career Benefits
We invest in our team by offering generous benefits for health and retirement, and professional development opportunities to grow your career. Additional information on benefits can be found on the [CalHR website](https://www.calhr.ca.gov/employees/pages/salary-and-benefits.aspx).
[](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm)
  * [Health](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm)
  * [Insurance](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm)
  * [Benefits](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm)
  * [Retirement](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm)


## Health
  * Medical
  * Dental 


  * Vision
  * Employee Assistance Program 


## Insurance
  * Life 
  * Group Legal 
  * Long Term Care 


  * Long Term Disability
  * State Disability 


## Benefits
  * Vacation and Sick Leave
  * Paid Holidays
  * Professional Development Days
  * Employee Wellness


  * Bilingual Pay Differential
  * Relocation Reimbursement
  * Commute Subsidy


## Retirement
  * CalPERS Retirement 


  * 401K & 457(b) Plans


### Local Impact 
Make a difference in your community with a career at DIR. We serve all of California with over 50 offices around the state. 
### Our Values
DIR’s [Core Values](https://dir.ca.gov/DirectorsOffice/Strategic-Plan.pdf) are essential to our mission to improve working conditions in California. DIR values accountability, integrity, compassion, diversity, service and innovation.
### A Workplace for All
At DIR, we cultivate a diverse and inclusive work environment that is reflective of the people we serve. We actively encourage a workplace in which all employees are respected and valued, can contribute their full and unique talents and are recognized for their contributions to DIR.
Team members have the opportunity to participate in groups such as our monthly **Diversity and Inclusion Committee** and **Disability Advisory Committee** meetings, events and more to help support our diverse work environment.
### Career Development 
DIR has openings for entry-level and experienced candidates to support our mission. Team members are encouraged to participate in training to improve their skills and career development. 
Careers for inspectors, investigators, analysts, supervisors and more are available in all of DIR’s activities, including: 
  * Workplace Safety Inspections 
  * Labor Law Investigations 
  * Assistance to Injured Workers
  * Registering Apprenticeship Programs
  * Attorneys, Judges, Hearing Reporters, Legal Support 
  * Office Support, Human Resources, Information Technology 
  * and more! 


[Back to top](https://dir.ca.gov/dirjobs/dirjobs.htm#top)
##  Current Openings 
[View all current DIR career openings on the Cal Careers website](https://www.jobs.ca.gov/CalHRPublic/Search/JobSearchResults.aspx#depid=83&view=Grouped)

  2. Paid Sick Leave in California


# Paid Sick Leave in California
[español](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Spanish/California-Paid-Sick-Leave.html)
In California, most workers earn Paid Sick Leave to take time off work to care for themself or a family member. Read about the different types of Paid Sick Leave below. 
## Paid Sick Leave (PSL)
Paid Sick Leave (PSL) is a permanent law in California that requires employers to provide paid time off to workers for treatment, diagnosis or preventative care for themselves, a family member or a designated person. Starting January 1, 2024, employers must provide at least 40 hours or five days off each year to most workers. This includes full-time, part-time and temporary workers who meet these qualifications:
  * Work for the same employer for at least 30 days within a year in California, and
  * Complete a 90-day employment period before taking any paid sick leave


PSL can be used to:
  * Recover from physical/mental illness or injury
  * To seek medical diagnosis, treatment, or preventative care
  * To care for a family member who is ill or needs medical diagnosis, treatment, or preventative care


Employers can choose to provide more PSL hours or days off. Employers can choose to have a PSL policy that provides all of the hours at one time, or the policy can require employees to earn the paid sick leave hours in an accrual plan. Employees under an accrual plan must earn at least one hour of paid sick leave for each 30 hours of work.   

  * Frequently Asked Questions on [Permanent Paid Sick Leave law that took effect in 2015](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Paid_Sick_Leave.htm)


No longer available: Supplemental Paid Sick Leave for COVID-19 (SPSL)
Supplemental Paid Sick Leave (SPSL) for COVID-19 was a law in 2021 and 2022 that required employers to provide additional paid time off for certain COVID-19 reasons. The law expired on December 31, 2022. [Read more about 2022 SPSL](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/COVID19Resources/2022-SPSL-FAQs.html) if you think you should have received this additional paid time off due to COVID-19 in 2021 or 2022. .
## Other Paid Leave Options
### Vacation Pay
  * There is no legal requirement in California that an employer provide either paid or unpaid vacation time. However, if an employer does have an paid vacation agreement, then certain restrictions are placed on the employer for fulling their obligation to provide vacation pay.  
Read more about [Vacation Pay](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/faq_vacation.htm). 


### Workers' Compensation 
  * Employers with one or more employees must maintain a valid workers' compensation insurance policy that pays for benefits for workers to recover from work-related injuries and illnesses.  
Read more about [workers' compensation for employers](https://dir.ca.gov/dwc/employer.htm) and [workers' compensation for employees](https://dir.ca.gov/dwc/InjuredWorker.htm). 


### Side by Side Comparison of Paid Leave Options
  * Paid Leave Options Chart provides an overview of paid leave laws that may cover California workers affected by illness or injury, including laws on paid family leave, paid sick leave and disability payments.  
[Paid Leave Options](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Leave-Rights-English-12-31-22-Extension.pdf) [Beneficios de permisos](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Leave-Rights-Spanish-12-31-22-Extension.pdf)

  1. [Labor Commissioner's Office](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/)


# Labor Commissioner's Office
The mission of the California Labor Commissioner's Office is to ensure a just day's pay in every workplace in the State and to promote economic justice through robust enforcement of labor laws. By combating wage theft, protecting workers from retaliation, and educating the public, we put earned wages into workers' pockets and help level the playing field for law-abiding employers. This office is also known as the Division of Labor Standards Enforcement (DLSE).
[California wildfires - FAQs on laws enforced by the Labor Commissioner's Office](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/FAQ-for-Wildfires.htm)
### [California Workplace Know Your Rights Act (SB 294)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Garment/New-Labor-Laws-in-California.html)
  * On or before February 1, 2026, and each year after that, an employer must provide employees a required workplace rights notice. The Labor Commissioner provides templates that can be used and meet the requirements of the law. The notice will be updated annually. The notice currently is available in [English](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-English.pdf), [Spanish](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Spanish.pdf), [Chinese (simplified)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Chinese-simple.pdf), [Chinese (traditional)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Chinese-Trad.pdf), [Vietnamese](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Vietnamese.pdf), [Korean](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Korean.pdf), [Tagalog](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Tagalog.pdf), [Hindi](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Hindi.pdf), [Punjabi](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Punjabi.pdf), [Arabic](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Arabic.pdf), and [Urdu](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Know-Your-Rights-Notice/Know-Your-Rights-Notice-Urdu.pdf). Click [here](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Garment/New-Labor-Laws-in-California.html) to learn more. 


**[Workers’ Rights Enforcement Grant Application& Information](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/Grants/Workers-Rights-Enforcement-Grant.html)**
**[Labor Commissioner’s Wage Theft Lawsuits against Uber& Lyft](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Lawsuits-Uber-Lyft.html)**
## [The Labor Commissioner is Hiring! ![DIR Jobs](https://dir.ca.gov/_images/icon_CareerOpptyAtDIR_blue.png)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Career-with-LCO.htm)
[en espanol](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/spanish/California-Minimum-Wage.htm)
### California Minimum Wage Effective January 1, 2026
Sixteen dollars and ninety-cents ($16.90) per hour for all hours worked, regardless of the size of the employer. 
[For more information on California minimum wage.](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/faq_minimumwage.htm)
### Sueldo mínimo de California a partir del 1 de enero de 2026
Dieciséis dólares y noventa centavos ($16.90) por hora por todas las horas trabajadas, independientemente del tamaño del empleador. 
[Para obtener más información sobre el salario mínimo de California.](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/faq_minimumwage.htm)
### Health Care Worker Minimum Wage FAQs Posted
Certain health care workers must be paid a higher minimum wage. The law went into effect October 16, 2024. 
For more information on the minimum wage increase and which workers are covered, visit the [Health Care Worker Minimum Wage FAQs](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Health-Care-Worker-Minimum-Wage-FAQ.htm). For more information on the clinic waiver program, visit the [Health Care Minimum Wage Clinic Waiver Program FAQs](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Health-Care-Worker-Minimum-Wage-Clinic-Waiver-FAQ.htm) and access the clinic waiver application [here](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Waiver-Application.pdf). 
### Fast Food Minimum Wage Effective April 1, 2024
Starting April 1, 2024, all “fast food restaurant employees” who are covered by the new law must be paid at least $20.00 per hour. For more information, visit [Fast Food Minimum Wage Frequently Asked Questions ](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Fast-Food-Minimum-Wage-FAQ.htm).
Fast food employers must post this Minimum Wage Order [Supplement for Fast Food Restaurant Employees](https://dir.ca.gov/iwc/MW-2024-FF-SUPPLEMENT.pdf).
### Supplemental Notice to H-2A Employees Effective March 15, 2024
[2810.5 Supplemental Notice to H-2A Employees - English](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/2810.5-Supplemental-Notice-to-H-2A-Employees_English.pdf)
[2810.5 Supplemental Notice to H-2A Employees - Spanish](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/2810.5-Supplemental-Notice-to-H-2A-Employees_Spanish.pdf)
### Licensed Manicurists Employment Status Information 
For more information, visit [Licensed Manicurists Frequently Asked Questions](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Licensed-Manicurists-FAQ.htm)
  * [Spanish](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Licensed-Manicurists-FAQ-Spanish.htm) [Vietnamese](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Licensed-Manicurists-FAQ-Vietnamese.htm) [Chinese Mandarin](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Licensed-Manicurists-FAQ-Chinese-Mandarin.htm) [Chinese Traditional](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Licensed-Manicurists-FAQ-Chinese-Traditional.htm) [Korean](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Licensed-Manicurists-FAQ-Korean.htm)


### SERVICE OF PROCESS
The Labor Commissioner’s Office has an email box to answer questions regarding service of lawsuits and writs of mandate on the Labor Commissioner’s Office. The email mailbox is LCOServiceRequests@dir.ca.gov. **Contacting the Labor Commissioner’s Office through the email mailbox does not constitute service of process. Rather, it is for answering your questions concerning service and providing you with information to help you effectuate service on the Labor Commissioner’s Office.** ”

  * Learn about [your rights ](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/Know_Your_Rights.html)as a worker

  * Obtain an [electrician's certification](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/ECU/ElectricalTrade.html)

### Public Meetings
Current schedule of meetings available for the public [Public Meetings](https://dir.ca.gov/directors_office_meetings.html).
**Help make pay equity the norm in California.[Tools and resources](https://women.ca.gov/californiapayequity/) for employers, employees and unions to comply with the Equal Pay Act are now available.**
### [Overtime Law for Agricultural Workers](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Overtime-for-Agricultural-Workers.html)
Effective January 1, 2022, agricultural workers employed by employers with 26 or more employees must receive overtime (1.5 times the employee’s regular rate of pay) for all hours worked over 8 hours in any workday or over 40 hours in any workweek. [AB 1066](https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billNavClient.xhtml?bill_id=201520160AB1066)
**[Employer Requirement to Notify Employees of Inspection by Immigration Agencies](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/Notice_to_Employee.html)**
[**Requisitos del empleador de avisar al empleado de inspección por agencias de inmigración**](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/Spanish/Notice_to_Employee.html)
[中文 (Chinese)](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/Chinese/Notice_to_Employee.html) [한국어 (Korean)](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/Korean/Notice_to_Employee.html) [Tagalog](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/Tagalog/Notice_to_Employee.html) [Tiếng Việt (Vietnamese)](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/Vietnamese/Notice_to_Employee.html)
### Entertainment Industry Sexual Harassment Prevention Training Requirements 
Beginning January 1, 2019, all talent agencies operating in California must provide their artists with educational materials on sexual harassment prevention, retaliation, reporting resources, nutrition and eating disorders. 
Applicants for entertainment work permits for minors between the ages of 14 and 17 must also complete sexual harassment prevention training before obtaining a minor’s entertainment work permit. 
  * More information on [sexual harassment prevention training requirements in the entertainment industry](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Entertainment-Industry-Sexual-Harassment-Prevention-Training-Requirements.html)


### Update: Sexual Violence and Harassment Prevention Training for Janitorial Service Providers
Due to the end of the COVID-19 public health emergency, the biennial in-person sexual violence and harassment prevention training that janitorial employers must provide to their nonsupervisory and supervisory workers may now be conducted safely. The list of qualified organizations is posted on this [website](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Qualified-Organization-Search.asp) as of January 1, 2024. Consequently, janitorial employers must begin compliance with the biennial in-person sexual violence and harassment prevention training requirements under Labor Code section 1429.5. Please visit the [sexual harassment prevention training page](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Janitorial-Training.html) for additional information regarding the required training materials. 
### Piece Rate Workers
If you are an employee who was paid on a piece rate basis during the period of July 1, 2012 through December 31, 2015, the Labor Commissioner’s office may have collected wages for you from your employer for rest and recovery periods and other nonproductive time. If you believe your employer has paid those wages to the Labor Commissioner on your behalf, please [complete this form](https://dir.ca.gov/PieceRateBackPayElection/AB1513form.pdf) and mail to the address below or take it to any [local office of the Labor Commissioner](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm). Please complete and submit a separate form for every employer who you think may have paid your wages to the Labor Commissioner.
Department of Industrial Relations  
AB 1513 Application  
Centralized Cashiering Unit  
2031 Howe Avenue, Suite 100  
Sacramento, CA 95825  

### Cómo Hacer un Reclamo al Fondo de Salario Impago
Si usted es un trabajador cuya paga entre el 1 de julio de 2012 y el 31 de diciembre de 2015 fue sobre la base de pago por pieza, el Comisionado Laboral puede haber recaudado sueldos de su empleador en concepto de períodos de descanso y otro tiempo improductivo. Si usted piensa que su paga ha sido enviada al Fondo de Sueldo Impago, por favor [complete este formulario](https://dir.ca.gov/PieceRateBackPayElection/AB1513form.pdf) y envíelo a la dirección que figura a continuación o entréguelo personalmente en cualquier [oficina del Comisionado Laboral](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Spanish/DistrictOffices.htm). Por favor complete y envíe un formulario por cada empleador que, a su juicio, pueda haber enviado su paga al Comisionado Laboral. 
Department of Industrial Relations  
AB 1513 Application  
Centralized Cashiering Unit  
2031 Howe Avenue, Suite 100  
Sacramento, CA 95825   

### Attention Talent Agents and Child Performer Services Permit Holders
Effective January 1, 2026, new legislation (AB 653) expands the list of mandated reporters to include talent agents, managers, and coaches working with minors under Penal Code Section 11165.7(a). Talent agents, managers, and coaches are required to report known or suspected child abuse. In accordance with this law, the Labor Commissioner will be including mandatory reporter statements with talent agent licenses and child performer services permits, as described in Penal Code Section 11166.5 subdivisions (a) and (b).
### ECU New Exam Scheduling Procedures
The Labor Commissioner’s Office is implementing new procedures for scheduling exams to be taken on or after June 1, 2026, administered by CPS HR Consulting. For more information, please refer to our website under [Test Taking Information](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/ECU/ECU_TestInfo.htm).

### Entertainment Industry Sexual Harassment Prevention Training Requirements
Beginning January 1, 2019, all talent agencies operating in California must provide their artists with educational materials on sexual harassment prevention, retaliation, reporting resources, nutrition and eating disorders. 
Applicants for entertainment work permits for minors between the ages of 14 and 17 must also complete sexual harassment prevention training before obtaining a minor’s entertainment work permit. 
  * More information on [sexual harassment prevention training requirements in the entertainment industry](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Entertainment-Industry-Sexual-Harassment-Prevention-Training-Requirements.html)


# Information on Minors and Employment
Almost all minors under the age of 18 are subject to California's child labor protections. Under the California Labor Code, "minor" is defined as any person under the age of 18 years required to attend school under the provisions of the Education Code, and any person under age six. "Dropouts" are subject to California's compulsory education laws, and thus are subject to all state child labor law requirements. Emancipated minors, while subject to all California's child labor laws, may apply for a work permit without their parents' permission.
* * *
## Child Labor Laws
The [Labor Commissioner's child labor law booklet](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/ChildLaborLawPamphlet.pdf) contains comprehensive information about child labor laws, school attendance, wage, hour, and age requirements, restrictions, employer requirements and work permits. It also includes references and links to the state [Labor Code](http://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codesTOCSelected.xhtml?tocCode=LAB&tocTitle=+Labor+Code+-+LAB), the [Education Code](http://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codesTOCSelected.xhtml?tocCode=EDC&tocTitle=+Education+Code+-+EDC) and other relevant laws and regulations.
A [table summarizing child labor law](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/MinorsSummaryCharts.pdf) requirements and restrictions categorized by age, and penalties for violating child labor laws is also posted for reference. 
## Work Permits
Except in limited circumstances defined in law and summarized in the [Child Labor Law Booklet](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/ChildLaborLawPamphlet.pdf), all minors under 18 years of age employed in the state of California must have a permit to work.
Prior to permitting a minor to work, employers must possess a valid [Permit to Employ and Work](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/dlseformB1-4.pdf). The Permit to Employ and Work are issued on the same form. A Permit to Employ and Work in industries other than entertainment is usually issued by an authorized person at the minor's school. During summer months or when school is not in session the work permit is obtained from the superintendent of the school district in which the minor resides.
Typically, after an employer agrees to hire a minor, the minor obtains from his or her school a Department of Education form entitled "[Statement of Intent to Employ Minor and Request for Work Permit](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/dlseformB1-1.pdf)". The form must be completed by the minor and the employer and signed by the minor's parent or guardian and the employer. After returning the completed and signed form to the school, school officials may issue the permit to employ and work.
Permits issued during the school year expire five days after the opening of the next succeeding school year and must be renewed.
## Entertainment Work Permits
Minors aged 15 days to 18 years employed in the [entertainment industry](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/ChildLaborLawPamphlet.pdf#page=39) must have a permit to work, and employers must have a permit to employ, both permits being issued by the [Labor Commissioner's Office](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm). These permits are also required for minors making phonographic recordings or who are employed as advertising or photographic models. Permits are required even when the entertainment is noncommercial in nature.
  * [Request an Entertainment Work Permit for a minor](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Entertainment-Work-Permit.htm)
  * [Entertainment Industry Sexual Harassment Prevention Training Requirements](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Entertainment-Industry-Sexual-Harassment-Prevention-Training-Requirements.html)
  * [Request Entertainment Permit to Employ Minors](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Entertainment-Permit-to-Employ-Minors.htm)
  * [Request a permit to provide services to a minor for the entertainment business](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Child_performer_services_permit.htm)

##  ![](https://dir.ca.gov/images/icons/icon_globe_large.png) Language Services at DIR
Close modal
The Department of Industrial Relations (DIR) recognizes the importance of communicating effectively with individuals, including those with limited English proficiency. DIR is making an effort to provide meaningful services for individuals that speak languages other than English.
## If you need to access information in a language other than English:

  2. DLSE Opinion Letters


# DLSE Opinion Letters
  * [DLSE Opinion letters (by subject)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/OpinionLetters-bySubject.htm)  
  

  * [DLSE Opinion letters (by date)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/OpinionLetters-byDate.htm)  
  

  * [Withdrawn opinion letters](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/OpinionLetters-Withdrawn.htm)


**PLEASE NOTE:** Labor Commissioner opinion letters posted on this website going forward will generally be accompanied by the letter that requested the issuance of the opinion. All future opinion letters will contain the following disclaimer, and will be based on the following express and implied terms:
**This opinion is based on the facts and circumstances described in your request and is given based on your representation, express or implied, that you have provided a full and fair description of all the facts and circumstances that would be pertinent to our consideration of the questions presented. Existence of any other factual or historical background not contained in your letter might require a conclusion different from the one expressed herein**.
**Cautionary Note:** All Wage Orders except Orders 14 and 17 contain references to specific Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) sections. These references are in the section of the Wage Orders that discuss their applicability. All the references relate to determining in employees meet the "Executive," ""Administrative," and/or "Professional" exemption.
Because the CFR sections referenced are those in effect when the Wage Orders were amended, and because some of the current CFRs have been amended, click on the link to access the correct 29 CFR language that is referenced in the [Wage Orders 1-13 and 15](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/29CFR-WOs1-13and15.pdf) or click on the following link to access the correct 29 CFR language that is referenced in [Wage Order 16](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/29CFR-WO16.pdf).
August 2024

# Contact the Labor Commissioner's Office
[español](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Spanish/DistrictOffices.htm)
[COVID-19 Remote and In-Person Office Operations](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Office-Notice-Corona.html)
For general information on the laws we enforce, please read our [frequently asked questions](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DLSE-FAQs.htm). You can also contact our offices by calling 833-LCO-INFO (833-526-4636).
For walk-in assistance, visit the office nearest you. Hours for walk-in assistance may vary by location, see table below for location and hours. Here you can find an office by [zip code](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/WageClaimOfficeSearch.asp).

**IMPORTANT:** please follow all COVID-19 prevention measures when visiting the Labor Commissioner’s offices.
## General COVID-19 Prevention Measures
**USE OF FACE COVERINGS**
Face covering are recommended while indoors regardless of vaccination status.
**COVID-19 SYMPTOMS**
If you are experiencing COVID-19 like symptoms or if you have been exposed or have been potentially exposed to COVID-19 and are either under isolation/quarantine orders or awaiting a COVID-19 test results, please do not appear in person.
COVID-19 symptoms include:
  1. Cough
  2. Shortness of breath or difficulty breathing
  3. Fatigue
  4. Sore throat
  5. Headache
  6. Fever
  7. Chills
  8. Muscle or body ache
  9. New loss of taste or smell
  10. Congestion or runny nose
  11. Nausea or vomiting
  12. Diarrhea


If you experience the symptoms mentioned above on the day of your scheduled appearance or 24 hours before, or if you have been exposed or have been potentially exposed to COVID-19 and are either under isolation/quarantine orders or awaiting a COVID-19 test results, you are asked to avoid entering the Labor Commissioner’s offices. Please contact our office to arrange to participate remotely or to request a continuance.
If a claim was closed for failure to appear to a scheduled conference or hearing due to an extraordinary circumstance or COVID-19 related reason, you may request that the claim be reopened.
California is issuing [regular updates on COVID-19](https://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CID/DCDC/Pages/Immunization/ncov2019.aspx), including [Coronavirus resources for California employers and workers](https://www.labor.ca.gov/coronavirus2019/) compiled by the Labor & Workforce Development Agency.
March 2026

# Electrician Certification Program
[español](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/ECU/Spanish/ElectricalTrade.html)
### ECU New Exam Scheduling Procedures
The Labor Commissioner’s Office is implementing new procedures for scheduling exams to be taken on or after June 1, 2026, administered by CPS HR Consulting. For more information, please refer to our website under [Test Taking Information](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/ECU/ECU_TestInfo.htm).
Existing law requires that persons performing work as electrician under a C-10 licensed contractor be certified pursuant to certification standards established by the Division of Labor Standards Enforcement. “Electricians” is defined as all persons who engage in the connection of electrical devices for electrical contractors licensed pursuant to Section 7058 of the Business and Profession Code, specifically, contractors classified as electrical contractors in the Contractors State License Board Rules and Regulations [Labor Code § 108 (c)].
### IMPORTANT NOTICE
Online applications for electrician certification and exams will be available on June 15, 2026. Electrician trainee online registration will be available at a later date. Electrician trainee applicants should continue to submit their application by mail until further notice. All printable applications will continue to be available on our website under [Electrician Certification Forms](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/ECU/ECUCertforms.htm).

# How to File a Wage Claim
[español](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Spanish/HowToFileWageClaim.htm)
If you have experienced [wage theft](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Examples_of_Wage_Theft.html) you can file an online wage claim with the Labor Commissioner's Office. 
No matter how you file your claim, the more information you can give us up front at the time of filing, the more effectively we can process your claim. Please do NOT use this button to file PAGA, retaliation, public works, or any other type of claim. This button will ONLY file a wage claim.
- If you need advocate assistance with your wage claim please refer to the following [list of community organizations](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/State-Wide-Referral-List.xlsx).
[File Wage or Garment Claim Online](https://cadir.my.site.com/oc) [Instructions for filing an online wage or garment claim](https://dir.ca.gov/DLSE/LCO-OWC-release-1-Training.pptx)
Workers in California have the right to file a wage claim when their employers do not pay them the wages or benefits they are owed. A wage claim starts the process to collect on those unpaid wages or benefits. Wage claims can be filed online, by [email](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm), mail or in person. California’s labor laws protect all workers, regardless of immigration status. 
## File your wage claim
  * File your wage claim
  * [Required documentation](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/dlseSupportingDocs.html#page_tabs)
  * [Your settlement conference](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/dlseSettlementConf.html#page_tabs)
  * [Wage claim hearing](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/dlseWageHearing.html#page_tabs)
  * [After the hearing](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/dlseAfterHearing.html#page_tabs)


  * When a wage claim is filed, the Labor Commissioner’s Office investigates the claim to determine if any wages or benefits are owed. In most cases, a settlement conference between the employee and employer is scheduled to resolve the issues. If the issues are not resolved at the conference, a hearing is scheduled so a hearing officer can review the evidence and make a decision on the claim. Learn more about the [wage claim process](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/policies.htm).
  
  
| ![photo of garment worker](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/HowToFileWageClaim_clip_image002.jpg)  |   
 | 
  * If you are a garment worker who has experienced wage theft, you must file your wage claim under the [Garment Worker Protection Act](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Garment_worker_wage_claim.html). 
 |  
| --- |  
 |  
  

  * The Labor Commissioner's Office has no jurisdiction over [independent contractors](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/FAQ_IndependentContractor.htm). Some workers who are misclassified as independent contractors are not provided basic labor rights. If you believe your employer is misclassifying you as an independent contractor, file a wage claim. The Labor Commissioner’s Office may hold a hearing to determine if a worker has been misclassified as an independent contractor.
  * If you work in the garment manufacturing industry, please see [this page](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Garment_worker_wage_claim.html) for how to file a garment worker wage claim.


## Make sure you file timely
  * Within**one year** for penalties regarding a bounced check or failing to provide access to, or a copy of, payroll or personnel records.
  * Within**two years** for an oral promise to pay more than minimum wage.
  * Within**three years** for violations of [minimum wage](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/faq_minimumwage.htm), [overtime](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/FAQ_Overtime.htm), unpaid [rest](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/FAQ_RestPeriods.htm) and [meal](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/FAQ_MealPeriods.htm) breaks, [sick leave](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Paid_Sick_Leave.htm), [illegal deductions](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/faq_deductions.htm) from pay or unpaid [reimbursements](http://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?sectionNum=2802.&lawCode=LAB)
  * Within **four years** for a written contract.


## Gather the facts
**Information on your employer**  
The Labor Commissioner’s Office will need the address and name of the company or individual that you work for in order to help you with your claim. The name of the company should be listed on paystubs, mailing labels, product labels, or if you cannot locate that information, you could write down their vehicle license plate number for the person who pays you. If you have more than one employer or supervisor, you might need to write down each of their names and vehicle license plate numbers.
**Other Responsible Parties**  
Any person acting as a supervisor or manager who violates workers’ labor rights can sometimes be held responsible in addition to the employer.
**Track all hours worked**  
Write down the time you begin and end work every day, when you take meal and rest breaks or heat recovery breaks, and the total hours you work.
If you are paid by contract or [piece rate](https://dir.ca.gov/pieceratebackpayelection/AB_1513_FAQs.htm), you should still earn at least the minimum wage for each hour worked. Keep track of the amount of time you spend on each contract or piece so that you can compare your total wages with the work you completed, to ensure you were paid for the work you did and that your pay before deductions equals at least the hourly minimum wage.
**Keep all your pay stubs**  
Each time you are paid, your employer must provide you with a [paystub](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/PayStub.pdf) ([piece rate pay stub](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/PayStubForPieceRate.pdf)) or detailed wage statement. This [itemized wage statement](http://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=LAB&sectionNum=226.) must contain the following information: your name, wages earned, dates of the pay period, your employer’s name, address and telephone number, as well as all deductions (taxes, etc.) and hours of [paid sick leave ](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Paid_Sick_Leave.htm)accrued.
All of the information and documents listed above will help you determine if you are paid properly, and will help support your case when filing a wage claim.
**Please note:** By law, your employer is required to keep accurate records of actual time worked. Your employer is also responsible for giving you an itemized wage statement each time you are paid. However, it is a good idea to keep track of your time and pay. The Labor Commissioner requests these documents if you have them available, because they could help the Labor Commissioner’s Office better understand your claim.
## How to file a claim
Once you have gathered the information about your employer and [documents if you have them available](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/dlseSupportingDocs.html#page_tabs), the next step in the process is to file a claim. You can file your claim by [email, mail or in person](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm), download a claim form, complete and print it, attach applicable documents and mail to the Labor Commissioner’s Office. 
The [claim forms](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Wage_Claim_forms.html) and instructions can be downloaded and printed in [multiple languages.](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Wage_Claim_forms.html)
You can also [File Wage or Garment Claim Online](https://cadir.my.site.com/oc)
If you need help filing your claim, visit or call a [Labor Commissioner's Office location](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm) near you. To locate the office nearest you refer to [the search by zip code](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/WageClaimOfficeSearch.asp).
﻿ 
For updates on your wage claim, please email the [district office](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm) where you filed your claim and **include your claim number in the subject line**. For general questions, please email DLSE2@dir.ca.gov.
## Resources for filing a wage claim
  * [Videos about the Wage Claim Process](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Videos-About-The-Wage-Claim-Process.htm)
  * [Contact the Labor Commissioner’s Office/locations](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm)
  * [Minimum Wage in California](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/faq_minimumwage.htm)
  * [Industrial Welfare Commission (IWC) Wage Orders](https://dir.ca.gov/iwc/wageorderindustries.htm)
  * [Which IWC Order? Classifications](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/WhichIWCOrderClassifications.pdf)
  * [Information for Subpoena (DLSE 564) (Rev. 1/09)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/forms/DLSE_Form_564-Info_for_Subpoena.pdf)
  * [Exemptions from the overtime laws](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/faq_overtimeexemptions.htm)
  * [DIR’s Privacy Policy](https://dir.ca.gov/od_pub/privacy.html)

# Judgment Enforcement Unit (JEU)
The Judgment Enforcement Unit (JEU) helps workers recover unpaid wages by enforcing judgments from the Labor Commissioner’s orders and decisions for unpaid wages and penalties.
### What is a Judgment?
When the Labor Commissioner’s Office determines that unpaid wages are due to workers, an Order, Decision or Award (ODA) or Notice of Findings (NOF) is issued. If the employer that owes the wages fails to pay the workers, a judgment is issued to help the worker recover the wages. 
These judgments are court orders to pay wages and other monies owed. Once the Labor Commissioner ODAs and NOFs are converted into a judgment, they can be enforced using the same mechanisms as any other civil judgment.
[Resources for Workers](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Resources-for-Workers.html)
[Judgments Referred to JEU](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Judgments-Referred-to-JEU.html)
[Identifying Assets](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Identifying-Assets.html)
### Judgment Success Stories
[Read more stories and cases](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Judgment-Success-Stories.html)
### Bank Levy Recovers Unpaid Wages for Restaurant Worker
![Bank-Levy](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/images/Bank-Levy.jpg)A restaurant worker in Thousand Oaks won over $20,000 in unpaid wages from a successful bank levy. The liable owner of the restaurant, singer and performer Siavash Shams, was the subject of the Judgment Enforcement Unit’s investigation...

  1. [Labor Commissioner's Office](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/)
  2. 2002 Division of Labor Standards Enforcement Policies and Interpretations Manual


# 2002 Division of Labor Standards Enforcement Policies and Interpretations Manual  
|   | 
##### **Instructions**
  1. This manual requires [Adobe Acrobat Reader](http://www.adobe.com/products/acrobat/readstep.html) to view and print.
  2. Links from the manual to the opinion letters will place you in a different document. To return to the manual, use the "back" button on your browser and not the "Go to Previous View" button on the Acrobat Reader toolbar.
  3. View the [DLSE Enforcement Manual](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DLSEManual/dlse_enfcmanual.pdf). (1 MB)


##### [Public Works Manual (6/14)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/PWManualCombined.pdf)
**Cautionary Note:** All Wage Orders except Orders 14 and 17 contain references to specific Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) sections. These references are in the section of the Wage Orders that discuss their applicability. All the references relate to determining in employees meet the "Executive," ""Administrative," and/or "Professional" exemption. Because the CFR sections referenced are those in effect when the Wage Orders were amended, and because some of the current CFRs have been amended, click on the link to access the correct 29 CFR language that is referenced in the [Wage Orders 1-13 and 15](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/29CFR-WOs1-13and15.pdf) or click on the following link to access the correct 29 CFR language that is referenced in [Wage Order 16](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/29CFR-WO16.pdf). Pursuant to Executive Order S-2-03, the DLSE opinion letters and the Enforcement Policies and Interpretations Manual are currently under review to determine their legal force and effect and to ensure compliance with the requirements of the [Administrative Procedures Act](http://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?sectionNum=11340.&lawCode=GOV). Please note, in addition, that DLSE opinion letters are advice in specific cases only. If you believe that a section of the Enforcement Policies and Interpretations Manual or an opinion letter needs to be reviewed to determine if it should go through the regulatory process pursuant to the [Administrative Procedures Act](http://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?sectionNum=11340.&lawCode=GOV), please submit your comments to dlsecomments@dir.ca.gov. Please include the specific manual section or opinion letter number and explain your specific concerns. The Division of Labor Standards Enforcement - Comments mailbox account (dlsecomments@dir.ca.gov) has been established solely to take comments on the enforcement manual and opinion letters. All comments will be read and considered, but no responses to questions or specific advice will be provided.  |  
| --- | --- |

The Department of Industrial Relations (DIR) recognizes the importance of communicating effectively with individuals, including those with limited English proficiency. DIR is making an effort to provide meaningful services for individuals that speak languages other than English.
## If you need to access information in a language other than English:
  * ### Use the Google Translate function below:
**Note:** This is a service provided by Google. Google Translate cannot translate all types of documents, and may not provide an exact translation.

# Registration Services
[español](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Spanish/Registration_Services.html)
### Update: Sexual Violence and Harassment Prevention Training for Janitorial Service Providers
Due to the end of the COVID-19 public health emergency, the biennial in-person sexual violence and harassment prevention training that janitorial employers must provide to their nonsupervisory and supervisory workers may now be conducted safely. The list of qualified organizations is posted on this [website](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Qualified-Organization-Search.asp) as of January 1, 2024. Consequently, janitorial employers must begin compliance with the biennial in-person sexual violence and harassment prevention training requirements under Labor Code section 1429.5. Please visit the [sexual harassment prevention training page](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Janitorial-Training.html) for additional information regarding the required training materials. 
### Qualified Organization Application
The Labor Commissioner’s Office is accepting applications for those interested in providing the Sexual Violence and Harassment Prevention Training for property service workers as required under Labor Code section 1429.5. Applications will be reviewed on a rolling basis. The application period for those interested in becoming a qualified organization is January 1st through June 30th of every year. The second application period for those interested in becoming a qualified organization is July 1st through December 31st of every year. If interested, please visit this [website](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Sexual-Violence-Harassment-Prevention-Training.html) for more information regarding the Qualified Organization Application process.
### Entertainment Industry Sexual Harassment Prevention Training Requirements
Beginning January 1, 2019, all talent agencies operating in California must provide their artists with training and education on sexual harassment prevention, retaliation, reporting resources, nutrition and eating disorders. 
Applicants for entertainment work permits for minors between the ages of 14 and 17 must also complete sexual harassment prevention training before obtaining a minor’s entertainment work permit. 
  * More information on [sexual harassment training prevention requirements in the entertainment industry](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Entertainment-Industry-Sexual-Harassment-Prevention-Training-Requirements.html)


### ECU New Exam Scheduling Procedures
The Labor Commissioner’s Office is implementing new procedures for scheduling exams to be taken on or after June 1, 2026. Please continue to visit our website for updates on the new procedures for scheduling. If you have questions, please email the Electrician Certification Unit at ECUInfo@dir.ca.gov or call us at (510) 286-3900. 
Pursuant to the California Labor Code, this unit administers exams, issues and renews permits, licenses, certifications, and registrations to the following:

# State Labor Law and Most Common Wage Violations Webinars
These interactive video webinars are presented online by the Division of Labor Standards Enforcement (DLSE).
**You will receive information from DLSE on:**
  * Wage & Hours Laws
  * Wage payment requirements
  * Common wage and hour violations
  * Wage payment requirements
  * Common wage and hour violations
  * Employer and employee rights and responsibilities
  * Paid Sick Leave & Covid-19
  * Employee vs. independent contractor


**_There is no charge for these seminars._** Reservations are highly recommended as the seminars are often full to capacity.

  1. [Labor Commissioner's Office](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/)
  2. Bureau of Field Enforcement (BOFE)


# Bureau of Field Enforcement (BOFE)
BOFE is responsible for investigation and enforcement of statutes covering workers' compensation insurance coverage, child labor, cash pay, unlicensed contractors, Industrial Welfare Commission orders, as well as group claims involving minimum wage and overtime claims.
  * [Overview of Bureau of Field Enforcement (2/2013)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/BOFE_Brochure.pdf)
  * [Report a labor law violation](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/HowToReportViolationtoBOFE.htm)
  * [Frequently asked questions regarding wage and hour laws](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DLSE-FAQs.htm)
  * [Phone information about your rights](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm)
  * [Líneas directas para información en español](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm)
  * [Time, Manner and Payment of Wages - English - Updated 10/13](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/LawsTimeMannerPaymentWages.pdf))
  * [Time, Manner and Payment of Wages - Spanish - Updated 10/13](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/LawsTimeMannerPaymentWagesSpanish.pdf))
  * [BOFE Annual Reports to the Legislature](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DLSEReports.htm%20)


The [Public Works Unit](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DLSE-PublicWorks.htm) investigates and enforces application of appropriate prevailing wage rates for public works construction projects.
The [Labor Employment Task Force (LETF)](https://dir.ca.gov/LETF/LETF.html) is a partnership of state and federal agencies, each expert in their own field, collaborating for vigorous and targeted enforcement against unscrupulous businesses participating in the "underground economy" historically abusing the workforce in the garment manufacturing, auto body, agriculture, car wash, construction, pallets and restaurant industries.
The [Sweatfree Code of Conduct](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DLSE-COC.htm) provides excerpts from the Public Contract Code section 6108 (g).

  1. [Labor Commissioner's Office](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/)
  2. Retaliation Complaint Investigation Unit (RCI)


# Retaliation Complaint Investigation Unit (RCI)
Employees and applicants for employment in California have the right to exercise their labor rights without retaliation or discrimination. The Labor Commissioner’s Office enforces [more than 45 labor laws that specifically prohibit discrimination and retaliation](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/HowToFileLinkCodeSections.htm), including [Equal Pay Act](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/California_Equal_Pay_Act.htm) violations. 
The Retaliation Complaint Investigation Unit (RCI) investigates [workplace retaliation complaints](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/HowToFileRetaliationComplaint.htm). Examples include:
  * Termination, suspension, transfer or demotion
  * Reduction in pay or hours
  * Disciplinary actions or threats
  * Unfair immigration-related practices


RCI also investigates:
  * pay inequity based on sex
  * pay inequity based on race
  * pay inequity based on ethnicity


[Information on how to file Retaliation Claim](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/HowToFileRetaliationComplaint.htm)
Workplace discrimination complaints based on race, color, ancestry, religion, age (40 and over), sex (including pregnancy), sexual orientation, marital status, or national origin (including language restrictions), should be filed with the [Department of Fair Employment and Housing](http://www.dfeh.ca.gov/).

  2. Wage Claim Adjudication


# Wage Claim Adjudication
DLSE adjudicates wage claims on behalf of workers who file claims for nonpayment of wages, overtime, or vacation pay, pursuant to California Labor Code sections 96 and 98. DLSE deputies hold informal conferences between employers and employees to resolve wage disputes. If a matter cannot be resolved at the informal conference, an administrative hearing is held to make a final determination on the matter.
**Note to Employers** : If you have non-negotiated checks on your books and the checks are made payable to employees who have left your employment and whom you cannot locate, you may send the Labor Commissioner the non-negotiated checks, along with an explanation of your efforts to contact the employee. The Labor Commissioner will try to locate the employee and make payment of the wages. If these attempts are unsuccessful, the checks will be deposited into the State of California Unclaimed Wages Fund. [Locate the nearest office of the Labor Commissioner](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/DistrictOffices.htm). 
  * [Information on how to file a wage claim and links to wage claim forms.](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/HowToFileWageClaim.htm)
  * [Information for Subpoena (DLSE 564) (Rev. 1/09)](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/forms/DLSE_Form_564-Info_for_Subpoena.pdf)
  * [Policies and procedures for wage claim processing -English](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Policies.htm)
  * [Policies and procedures for wage claim processing -Spanish](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/PoliciesSpanish.pdf)

**Cautionary Note:** All Wage Orders except Orders 14 and 17 contain references to specific Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) sections. These references are in the section of the Wage Orders that discuss their applicability. All the references relate to determining whether employees meet the "Executive," ""Administrative," and/or "Professional" exemption.
The CFR sections referenced are those in effect when the Wage Orders were amended, and some of the CFRs have been amended since then. Click on the link to access the correct 29 CFR language that is referenced in the [Wage Orders 1-13 and 15](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/29CFR-WOs1-13and15.pdf) or click on the following link to access the correct 29 CFR language that is referenced in [Wage Order 16](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/29CFR-WO16.pdf).

# Minimum Wage
[español](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Spanish/minimum_wage.htm)
The minimum wage in California, effective January 1, 2026, is $16.90/hour for all employers. Fast Food Restaurant employers, effective April 1, 2024, and Healthcare Facility employers, effective October 16, 2024 (see below), have a higher minimum wage.   
  
Some cities and counties have higher minimum wages than the state’s rate. Here is a [list of City and County minimum wages in California](https://laborcenter.berkeley.edu/inventory-of-us-city-and-county-minimum-wage-ordinances/#s-2) maintained by UC Berkeley.  
  
Most employees in California must be paid at least the minimum wage per hour, with some exceptions explained in the FAQs below.
  * [Frequently Asked Questions about exemption and pay](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/faq_minimumwage.htm)
  * [California’s Minimum Wage to Increase to $16.90 per hour in January 2026](https://dir.ca.gov/DIRNews/2025/2025-118.html)
  * [Frequently Asked Questions about the 2017-2023 $15 phase in](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/SB3_FAQ.htm)


## Fast Food Minimum Wage Effective April 1, 2024
Starting April 1, 2024, all “fast food restaurant employees” who are covered by the new law must be paid at least $20.00 per hour. For more information, visit [Fast Food Minimum Wage Frequently Asked Questions](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Fast-Food-Minimum-Wage-FAQ.htm). Fast food employers must post this Minimum Wage Order [Supplement for Fast Food Restaurant Employees](https://dir.ca.gov/iwc/MW-2024-FF-SUPPLEMENT.pdf). 
## Health Care Worker Minimum Wage FAQs Posted
Starting October 16, 2024, certain health care workers must be paid a higher minimum wage. 
For more information on the minimum wage increase and which workers are covered, visit the [Health Care Worker Minimum Wage FAQs](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Health-Care-Worker-Minimum-Wage-FAQ.htm). For more information on the clinic waiver program, visit the [Health Care Minimum Wage Clinic Waiver Program FAQs](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Health-Care-Worker-Minimum-Wage-Clinic-Waiver-FAQ.htm) and access the clinic waiver application [here](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Waiver-Application.pdf). 
December 2025

# Outreach
## Reaching EVERY Californian Campaign
Protecting workers is more than a statutory obligation for the Labor Commissioner’s Office (LCO). Our mission includes protecting the most vulnerable workers from labor law violations and helping law-abiding employers with unfair competition. If workers don’t know their labor rights, they cannot exercise them. The Reaching Every Californian public awareness campaign focuses on underserved worker populations where labor law violations occur more frequently.  
  
This comprehensive, action-driven outreach program highlights the critical role workers and employers have in exposing exploitative working conditions that undercut law-abiding employers. This program provides tools to workers and employers so they can identify and report labor law violations and employers who intentionally skirt the law. 
The _Reaching Every Californian_ campaign builds pathways to communities that do not have an existing connection to the LCO within their family network or community resources. This campaign brings the LCO to the community through clinics, workshops, partnerships and multimedia messages.
###  [For Employers](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/outreach.html#BEP)  
[For Workers](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/outreach.html#WRAP)  
[For Young People](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/outreach.html#YAP)  
[Resources](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/outreach.html#Resources)  
[Videos](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/outreach.html#Videos)
## Business Engagement Program (BEP)
![Business  Engagement Program](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/images/BEP-JPG.jpg)
When a worker is denied workplace protections, it creates unfair competition for responsible employers. The Business Engagement Program (BEP) promotes fair and legal business practices and helps stakeholders in the fight to eliminate unfair competition. Employers are critical partners in identifying cases where law-abiding companies are systematically denied a level playing field.
The Labor Commissioner’s Office is a resource for employers committed to complying with the law, in particular small employers who are a critical part of California’s economy.
This program assists employers in understanding workplace protections and obligations. BEP began in 2020 and has quickly become a key resource for employers trying to better understand California labor laws. Our office reaches the business community by partnering with employers and associations to provide free workshops and presentations tailored to meet their needs.
Please contact us at MakeItFair@dir.ca.gov if you have questions or you are interested in receiving a training on workplace laws.
  * [Brochure for the Business Engagement Program](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/BEP_Brochure_English-AF.pdf)
  * [Brochure for the Business Engagement Program - Spanish](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/BEP_Brochure_Spanish-AF.pdf)


## Workplace Rights Ambassador Program (WRAP) 
![Workplace  Rights Ambassador Program](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/images/WRAP-Logo.jpg)
All Californians have workplace rights to protect their pay and improve working conditions. The Labor Commissioner’s Office is committed to making workplace protections known to everyone, including workers who may have obstacles in obtaining information due to traumatic life experiences. Those traumatic experiences impact workers who have experienced sexual assault, harassment or domestic violence, young workers who have come through the foster system and workers who may have difficulty accessing services due to language barriers, lack of trust or other negative experiences with government. 
The Workplace Rights Ambassador Project (WRAP) is an initiative that seeks to build bridges between the Labor Commissioner’s Office and workers who have experienced trauma. This trauma-informed approach recognizes many workers have barriers to accessing rights and protections. 
WRAP prioritizes relationships with community organizations that are trusted resources for workers who have experienced trauma that deter them from exercising their rights at work. By investing in trusted community leaders who are providing other support services, the Labor Commissioner’s Office can reach workers who may not have otherwise learned about their rights. 
Please contact us at mramirez@dir.ca.gov with your questions or if you are interested in receiving a training on workplace rights. 
  * [Brochure for the Workplace Rights Ambassador Program](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/WRAP-Brochure-English-AF.pdf)
    * [Brochure for the Workplace Rights Ambassador Program Spanish](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/WRAP-Brochure-Spanish-AF.pdf)
  * [Victims of Crimes Brochure](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Victims-of-Crimes-Brochure.pdf)


## Youth Advocate Program
![Youth Advocate Program](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/images/YAP-Logo.jpg)
Through our Youth Advocate Program (YAP), the Labor Commissioner’s Office is investing in reaching young workers. In many families among the working poor, high-school-age children often serve as interpreters for monolingual parents and provide support when parents experience wage theft and retaliation. This program focuses on reaching young workers and providing them with tools to advocate for themselves and their relatives at work.   
  
Wage theft happens to all types of workers, especially those who are unaware of their rights. As students and young adults seek employment for the first time, they are more susceptible to wage theft by employers who don’t follow the law. In collaboration with our education partners, the Youth Advocate Program at the Labor Commissioner’s Office will help prepare California’s future workers.  
  
Please contact us at YAP@dir.ca.gov with your questions or if you are interested in participating in YAP. 
  * [Youth Advocate Brochure](https://dir.ca.gov/dlse/Youth-Advocate-Brochure.pdf)

California Workplace - Know Your Rights 
On or before **_February 1, 2026_** , and each year after that, an employer must provide employees a required workplace rights notice. The Labor Commissioner provides these templates that can be used and meet the requirements of the law. The notice will be updated annually. The notice currently is available in English and Spanish, and soon will be available in additional languages.
